15	
16	The stream would be their battleground.
17	
18	Septon Sefton waddled out and said a prayer, beseeching the Father Above to look down on these two men and judge them justly, asking the Warrior to lend his strength to the man whose cause was just and true, begging the Mother's mercy for the liar, that he might be forgiven for his sins. When the praying was over and done with, he turned to Ser Eustace Osgrey one last time. "Ser," he said, "I beg you once again, withdraw your accusation."
19	
20	"I will not," the old man said, his mustache trembling.
21	
22	The fat septon turned to Lady Rohanne. "Good-sister, if you did this thing, confess your guilt, and offer good Ser Eustace some restitution for his wood. Elsewise blood must flow."
23	
24	"My champion will prove my innocence before the eyes of gods and men."
25	
26	"Trial by battle is not the only way," said the septon, waist-deep in the water. "Let us go to Goldengrove, I implore you both, and place the matter before Lord Rowan for his judgment."
27	
28	"Never," said Ser Eustace. The Red Widow shook her head.
29	
30	Ser Lucas Inchfield looked at Lady Rohanne, his face dark with fury. "You will marry me when this mummer's farce is done. As your lord father wished."
31	
32	"My lord father never knew you as I do," she gave back.
33	
34	Dunk went to one knee beside Egg, and put the signet back in the boy's hand; four three-headed dragons, two and two, the arms of Maekar, Prince of Summerhall. "Back in the boot," he said, "but if it happens that I die, go to the nearest of your father's friends and have him take you back to Summerhall.
35	
36	Don't try to cross the whole Reach on your own. See you don't forget, or my ghost will come and clout you in the ear."
37	
38	"Yes, ser," said Egg, "but I'd sooner you didn't die."
39	
40	"It's too hot to die." Dunk donned his helm, and Egg helped him fasten it tightly to his gorget. The blood was sticky on his face, though Ser Eustace had torn a piece off his cloak to help stop the gash from bleeding. He rose and went to Thunder. Most of the smoke had blown away, he saw as he swung up onto the saddle, but the sky was still dark. Clouds, he thought, dark clouds. It had been so long. Maybe it's an omen. But is it his omen, or mine? Dunk was no good with omens.
41	
42	Across the stream, Ser Lucas had mounted up as well. His horse was a chestnut courser; a splendid animal, swift and strong, but not as large as Thunder. What the horse lacked in size he made up for in armor, though; he was clad in crinet, chanfron, and a coat of light chain. The Longinch himself wore black enameled plate and silvery ringmail. An onyx spider squatted malignantly atop his helmet, but his shield displayed his own arms: a bend sinister, chequy black and white, on a pale gray field. Dunk watched Ser Lucas hand it to a squire. He does not mean to use it. When another squire delivered him a poleax, he knew why. The ax was long and lethal, with a banded haft, a heavy head, and a wicked spike on its back, but it was a two-handed weapon. The Longinch would need to trust in his armor to protect him. I need to make him rue that choice.
43	
44	His own shield was on his left arm, the shield Tanselle had painted with his elm and falling star. A child's rhyme echoed in his head. Oak and iron, guard me well, or else I'm dead, and doomed to hell. He slid his longsword from its scabbard. The weight of it felt good in his hands.
45	
46	He put his heels into Thunder's flanks and walked the big destrier down into the water. Across the stream, Ser Lucas did the same. Dunk pressed right, so as to present the Longinch with his left side, protected by his shield. That was not something Ser Lucas was willing to concede him. He turned his courser quickly, and they came together in a tumult of gray steel and green spray. Ser Lucas struck with his poleax. Dunk had to twist in the saddle to catch it on his shield. The force of it shot down his arm and jarred his teeth together. He swung his sword in answer, a sideways cut that took the other knight beneath his upraised arm. Steel screamed on steel, and it was on.
47	
48	The Longinch spurred his courser in a circle, trying to get around to Dunk's unprotected side, but Thunder wheeled to meet him, snapping at the other horse. Ser Lucas delivered one crashing blow after another, standing in his stirrups to get all his weight and strength behind the axhead. Dunk shifted his shield to catch each blow as it came. Half crouched beneath its oak, he hacked at Inchfield's arms and side and legs, but his plate turned every stroke. Around they went, and around again, the water lapping at their legs. The Longinch attacked, and Dunk defended, watching for a weakness.
49	
50	Finally he saw it. Every time Ser Lucas lifted his ax for another blow, a gap appeared beneath his arm.
51	
52	There was mail and leather there, and padding underneath, but no steel plate. Dunk kept his shield up, trying to time his attack. Soon. Soon. The ax crashed down, wrenched free, came up. Now! He slammed his spurs into Thunder, driving him closer, and thrust with his longsword, to drive his point through the opening.
53	
54	But the gap vanished as quick as it had appeared. His swordpoint scraped a rondel, and Dunk, overextended, almost lost his seat. The ax descended with a crash, slanting off the iron rim of Dunk's shield, crunching against the side of his helm, and striking Thunder a glancing blow along the neck.
55	
56	The destrier screamed and reared up on two legs, his eye rolling white in pain as the sharp coppery smell of blood filled the air. He lashed out with his iron hooves just as the Longinch was moving in. One caught Ser Lucas in the face, the other on a shoulder. Then the heavy warhorse came down atop his courser.
57	
58	It all happened in a heartbeat. The two horses went down in a tangle, kicking and biting at each other, churning up the water and the mud below. Dunk tried to throw himself from the saddle, but one foot tangled in a stirrup. He fell face first, sucking down one desperate gulp of air before the stream came rushing into the helm through the eyeslit. His foot was still caught up, and he felt a savage yank as Thunder's struggles almost pulled his leg out of its socket. Just as quickly he was free, turning, sinking.
59	
60	For a moment he flailed helplessly in the water. The world was blue and green and brown.
61	
62	The weight of his armor pulled him down until his shoulder bumped the streambed. If that is down the other way is up. Dunk's steel-clad hands fumbled at the stones and sands, and somehow he gathered his legs up under him and stood. He was reeling, dripping mud, with water pouring from the breath holes in his dinted helm, but he was standing. He sucked down air.
63	
64	His battered shield still clung to his left arm, but his scabbard was empty and his sword was gone. There was blood inside his helm as well as water. When he tried to shift his weight, his ankle sent a lance of pain right up his leg. Both horses had struggled back to their feet, he saw. He turned his head, squinting one-eyed through a veil of blood, searching for his foe. Gone, he thought, he's drowned, or Thunder crushed his skull in.
65	
66	Ser Lucas burst up out of the water right in front of him, sword in hand. He struck Dunk's neck a savage blow, and only the thickness of his gorget kept his head upon his shoulders. He had no blade to answer with, only his shield. He gave ground, and the Longinch came after, screaming and slashing. Dunk's upraised arm took a numbing blow above the elbow. A cut to his hip made him grunt in pain. As he backed away, a rock turned beneath his foot, and he went down to one knee, chest-high in the water.
67	
68	He got his shield up, but this time Ser Lucas struck so hard he split the thick oak right down the middle, and drove the remnants back into Dunk's face. His ears were ringing and his mouth was full of blood, but somewhere far away he heard Egg screaming. "Get him, ser, get him, get him, he's right there! "
69	
70	Dunk dived forward. Ser Lucas had wrenched his sword free for another cut. Dunk slammed into him waist-high and knocked him off his feet. The stream swallowed both of them again, but this time Dunk was ready. He kept one arm around the Longinch and forced him to the bottom. Bubbles came streaming out from behind Inchfield's battered, twisted visor, but still he fought. He found a rock at the bottom of the stream and began hammering at Dunk's head and hands. Dunk fumbled at his swordbelt.
71	
72	Have I lost the dagger too? he wondered. No, there it was. His hand closed around the hilt and he wrenched it free, and drove it slowly through the churning water, through the iron rings and boiled leather beneath the arm of Lucas the Longinch, turning it as he pushed. Ser Lucas jerked and twisted, and the strength left him. Dunk shoved away and floated. His chest was on fire. A fish flashed past his face, long and white and slender. What's that? he wondered. What's that? What's that?
73	
74	He woke in the wrong castle.
75	
76	When his eyes opened, he did not know where he was. It was blessedly cool. The taste of blood was in his mouth and he had a cloth across his eyes, a heavy cloth fragrant with some unguent. It smelled of cloves, he thought.
77	
78	Dunk groped at his face, pulled the cloth away. Above him torchlight played against a high ceiling.
79	
80	Ravens were walking on the rafters overhead, peering down with small black eyes and quork ing at him. I am not blind, at least. He was in a maester's tower. The walls were lined with racks of herbs and potions in earthen jars and vessels of green glass. A long trestle table nearby was covered with parchments, books, and queer bronze instruments, all spattered with droppings from the ravens in the rafters. He could hear them muttering at one another.
81	
82	He tried to sit. It proved a bad mistake. His head swam, and his left leg screamed in agony when he put the slightest weight upon it. His ankle was wrapped in linen, he saw, and there were linen strips around his chest and shoulders, too.
83	
84	"Be still." A face appeared above him, young and pinched, with dark brown eyes on either side of a hooked nose. Dunk knew that face. The man who owned it was all in gray, with a chain collar hanging loose about his neck, a maester's chain of many metals. Dunk grabbed him by the wrist. "Where? . . ."
85	
86	"Coldmoat," said the maester. "You were too badly injured to return to Standfast, so Lady Rohanne commanded us to bring you here. Drink this." He raised a cup of . . . something . . . to Dunk's lips. The potion had a bitter taste, like vinegar, but at least it washed away the taste of blood.
87	
88	Dunk made himself drink it all. Afterward he flexed the fingers of his sword hand, and then the other. At least my hands still work, and my arms. "What . . . what did I hurt?"
89	
90	"What not?" The maester snorted. "A broken ankle, a sprained knee, a broken collarbone, bruising . . . your upper torso is largely green and yellow and your right arm is a purply black. I thought your skull was cracked as well, but it appears not. There is that gash in your face, ser. You will have a scar, I fear. Oh, and you had drowned by the time we pulled you from the water."
91	
92	"Drowned?" said Dunk.
93	
94	"I never suspected that one man could swallow so much water, not even a man as large as you, ser.
95	
96	Count yourself fortunate that I am ironborn. The priests of the Drowned God know how to drown a man and bring him back, and I have made a study of their beliefs and customs."
97	
98	I drowned. Dunk tried to sit again, but the strength was not in him. I drowned in water that did not even come up to my neck. He laughed, then groaned in pain. "Ser Lucas?"
99	
100	"Dead. Did you doubt it?"
101	
102	No. Dunk doubted many things, but not that. He remembered how the strength had gone out of the Longinch's limbs, all at once. "Egg," he got out. "I want Egg."
103	
104	"Hunger is a good sign," the maester said, "but it is sleep you need just now, not food."
105	
106	Dunk shook his head, and regretted it at once. "Egg is my squire . . ."
107	
108	"Is he? A brave lad, and stronger than he looks. He was the one to pull you from the stream. He helped us get that armor off you, too, and rode with you in the wayn when we brought you here. He would not sleep himself, but sat by your side with your sword across his lap, in case someone tried to do you harm.
109	
110	He even suspected me , and insisted that I taste anything I meant to feed you. A queer child, but devoted."
111	
112	"Where is he?"
113	
114	"Ser Eustace asked the boy to attend him at the wedding feast. There was no one else on his side. It would have been discourteous for him to refuse."
115	
116	"Wedding feast?" Dunk did not understand.
117	
118	"You would not know, of course. Coldmoat and Standfast were reconciled after your battle. Lady Rohanne begged leave of old Ser Eustace to cross his land and visit Addam's grave, and he granted her that right. She knelt before the blackberries and began to weep, and he was so moved that he went to comfort her. They spent the whole night talking of young Addam and my lady's noble father. Lord Wyman and Ser Eustace were fast friends, until the Blackfyre Rebellion. His lordship and my lady were wed this morning, by our good Septon Sefton. Eustace Osgrey is the lord of Coldmoat, and his chequy lion flies beside the Webber spider on every tower and wall."
119	
120	Dunk's world was spinning slowly all around him. That potion. He's put me back to sleep. He closed his eyes, and let all the pain drain out of him. He could hear the ravens quork ing and screaming at each other, and the sound of his own breath, and something else as well . . . a softer sound, steady, heavy, somehow soothing. "What's that?" he murmured sleepily. "That sound? . . ."
121	
122	"That?" The maester listened. "That's just rain."
123	
124	He did not see her till the day they took their leave.
125	
126	"This is folly, ser," Septon Sefton complained, as Dunk limped heavily across the yard, swinging his splinted foot and leaning on a crutch. "Maester Cerrick says you are not half healed as yet, and this rain . . . you're like to catch a chill, if you do not drown again. At least wait for the rain to stop."
127	
128	"That may be years." Dunk was grateful to the fat septon, who had visited him near every day . . . to pray for him, ostensibly, though more time seemed to be taken up with tales and gossip. He would miss his loose and lively tongue and cheerful company, but that changed nothing. "I need to go."
129	
130	The rain was lashing down around them, a thousand cold gray whips upon his back. His cloak was already sodden. It was the white wool cloak Ser Eustace had given him, with the green-and-gold-checkered border. The old knight had pressed it on him once again, as a parting gift.
131	
132	"For your courage and leal service, ser," he said. The brooch that pinned the cloak at his shoulder was a gift as well; an ivory spider brooch with silver legs. Clusters of crushed garnets made spots upon its back.
133	
134	"I hope this is not some mad quest to hunt down Bennis," Septon Sefton said. "You are so bruised and battered that I would fear for you, if that one found you in such a state."
135	
136	Bennis, Dunk thought bitterly, bloody Bennis . While Dunk had been making his stand at the stream, Bennis had tied up Sam Stoops and his wife, ransacked Standfast from top to bottom, and made off with every item of value he could find, from candles, clothes, and weaponry to Osgrey's old silver cup and a small cache of coin the old man had hidden in his solar behind a mildewed tapestry. One day Dunk hoped to meet Ser Bennis of the Brown Shield again, and when he did . . . "Bennis will keep."
137	
138	"Where will you go?" The septon was panting heavily. Even with Dunk on a crutch, he was too fat to match his pace.
139	
140	"Fair Isle. Harrenhal. The Trident. There are hedges everywhere." He shrugged. "I've always wanted to see the Wall."
141	
142	"The Wall?" The septon jerked to a stop. "I despair of you, Ser Duncan!" he shouted, standing in the mud with outspread hands as the rain came down around him. "Pray, ser, pray for the Crone to light your way!" Dunk kept walking.
143	
144	She was waiting for him inside the stables, standing by the yellow bales of hay in a gown as green as summer. "Ser Duncan," she said when he came pushing through the door. Her red braid hung down in front, the end of it brushing against her thighs. "It is good to see you on your feet."
145	
146	You never saw me on my back, he thought. "M'lady. What brings you to the stables. It's a wet day for a ride."
147	
148	"I might say the same to you."
149	
150	"Egg told you?" I owe him another clout in the ear.
151	
152	"Be glad he did, or I would have sent men after you to drag you back. It was cruel of you to try and steal away without so much as a farewell."
153	
154	She had never come to see him while he was in Maester Cerrick's care, not once. "That green becomes you well, m'lady," he said. "It brings out the color of your eyes." He shifted his weight awkwardly on the crutch. "I'm here for my horse."
155	
156	"You do not need to go. There is a place for you here, when you're recovered. Captain of my guards.
157	
158	And Egg can join my other squires. No one need ever know who he is."
159	
160	"Thank you, m'lady, but no." Thunder was in a stall a dozen places down. Dunk hobbled toward him.
161	
162	"Please reconsider, ser. These are perilous times, even for dragons and their friends. Stay until you've healed." She walked along beside him. "It would please Lord Eustace, too. He is very fond of you."
163	
164	"Very fond," Dunk agreed. "If his daughter weren't dead, he'd want me to marry her. Then you could be my lady mother. I never had a mother, much less a lady mother."
165	
166	For half a heartbeat Lady Rohanne looked as though she was going to slap him again. Maybe she'll just kick my crutch away.
167	
168	"You are angry with me, ser," she said instead. "You must let me make amends."
169	
170	"Well," he said, "you could help me saddle Thunder."
171	
172	"I had something else in mind." She reached out her hand for his, a freckled hand, her fingers strong and slender. I'll bet she's freckled all over. "How well do you know horses?"
173	
174	"I ride one."
175	
176	"An old destrier bred for battle, slow-footed and ill-tempered. Not a horse to ride from place to place."
177	
178	"If I need to get from place to place, it's him or these." Dunk pointed at his feet.
179	
180	"You have large feet," she observed. "Large hands as well. I think you must be large all over. Too large for most palfreys. They'd look like ponies with you perched upon their backs. Still, a swifter mount would serve you well. A big courser, with some Dornish sand steed for endurance." She pointed to the stall across from Thunder's. "A horse like her."
181	
182	She was a blood bay with a bright eye and a long fiery mane. Lady Rohanne took a carrot from her sleeves and stroked her head as she took it. "The carrot, not the fingers," she told the horse, before she turned again to Dunk. "I call her Flame, but you may name her as you please. Call her Amends, if you like."
183	
184	For a moment he was speechless. He leaned on the crutch and looked at the blood bay with new eyes.
185	
186	She was magnificent. A better mount than any the old man had ever owned. You had only to look at those long, clean limbs to see how swift she'd be.
187	
188	"I bred her for beauty, and for speed."
189	
190	He turned back to Thunder. "I cannot take her."
191	
192	"Why not?"
193	
194	"She is too good a horse for me. Just look at her."
195	
196	A flush crept up Rohanne's face. She clutched her braid, twisting it between her fingers. "I had to marry, you know that. My father's will . . . oh, don't be such a fool."
197	
198	"What else should I be? I'm thick as a castle wall and bastard born as well."
199	
200	"Take the horse. I refuse to let you go without something to remember me by."
201	
202	"I will remember you, m'lady. Have no fear of that."
203	
204	"Take her!"
205	
206	Dunk grabbed her braid and pulled her face to his. It was awkward with the crutch and the difference in their heights. He almost fell before he got his lips on hers. He kissed her hard. One of her hands went around his neck, and one around his back. He learned more about kissing in a moment than he had ever known from watching. But when they finally broke apart, he drew his dagger. "I know what I want to remember you by, m'lady."
207	
208	Egg was waiting for him at the gatehouse, mounted on a handsome new sorrel palfrey and holding Maester's lead. When Dunk trotted up to them on Thunder, the boy looked surprised. "She said she wanted to give you a new horse, ser."
209	
210	"Even highborn ladies don't get all they want," Dunk said, as they rode out across the drawbridge. "It wasn't a horse I wanted." The moat was so high it was threatening to overflow its banks. "I took something else to remember her by instead. A lock of that red hair." He reached under his cloak, brought out the braid, and smiled.
211	
212	In the iron cage at the crossroads, the corpses still embraced. They looked lonely, forlorn. Even the flies had abandoned them, and the crows as well. Only some scraps of skin and hair remained upon the dead men's bones.
213	
214	Dunk halted, frowning. His ankle was hurting from the ride, but it made no matter. Pain was as much a part of knighthood as were swords and shields. "Which way is south?" he asked Egg. It was hard to know, when the world was all rain and mud and the sky was gray as a granite wall.
215	
216	"That's south, ser." Egg pointed. "That's north."
217	
218	"Summerhall is south. Your father."
219	
220	"The Wall is north."
221	
222	Dunk looked at him. "That's a long way to ride."
223	
224	"I have a new horse, ser."
225	
226	"So you do." Dunk had to smile. "And why would you want to see the Wall?"
227	
228	"Well," said Egg. "I hear it's tall."
229

The stream would be their battleground.

Septon Sefton waddled out and said a prayer, beseeching the Father Above to look down on these two men and judge them justly, asking the Warrior to lend his strength to the man whose cause was just and true, begging the Mother's mercy for the liar, that he might be forgiven for his sins. When the praying was over and done with, he turned to Ser Eustace Osgrey one last time. "Ser," he said, "I beg you once again, withdraw your accusation."

"I will not," the old man said, his mustache trembling.

The fat septon turned to Lady Rohanne. "Good-sister, if you did this thing, confess your guilt, and offer good Ser Eustace some restitution for his wood. Elsewise blood must flow."

"My champion will prove my innocence before the eyes of gods and men."

"Trial by battle is not the only way," said the septon, waist-deep in the water. "Let us go to Goldengrove, I implore you both, and place the matter before Lord Rowan for his judgment."

"Never," said Ser Eustace. The Red Widow shook her head.

Ser Lucas Inchfield looked at Lady Rohanne, his face dark with fury. "You will marry me when this mummer's farce is done. As your lord father wished."

"My lord father never knew you as I do," she gave back.

Dunk went to one knee beside Egg, and put the signet back in the boy's hand; four three-headed dragons, two and two, the arms of Maekar, Prince of Summerhall. "Back in the boot," he said, "but if it happens that I die, go to the nearest of your father's friends and have him take you back to Summerhall.

Don't try to cross the whole Reach on your own. See you don't forget, or my ghost will come and clout you in the ear."

"Yes, ser," said Egg, "but I'd sooner you didn't die."

"It's too hot to die." Dunk donned his helm, and Egg helped him fasten it tightly to his gorget. The blood was sticky on his face, though Ser Eustace had torn a piece off his cloak to help stop the gash from bleeding. He rose and went to Thunder. Most of the smoke had blown away, he saw as he swung up onto the saddle, but the sky was still dark. Clouds, he thought, dark clouds. It had been so long. Maybe it's an omen. But is it his omen, or mine? Dunk was no good with omens.

Across the stream, Ser Lucas had mounted up as well. His horse was a chestnut courser; a splendid animal, swift and strong, but not as large as Thunder. What the horse lacked in size he made up for in armor, though; he was clad in crinet, chanfron, and a coat of light chain. The Longinch himself wore black enameled plate and silvery ringmail. An onyx spider squatted malignantly atop his helmet, but his shield displayed his own arms: a bend sinister, chequy black and white, on a pale gray field. Dunk watched Ser Lucas hand it to a squire. He does not mean to use it. When another squire delivered him a poleax, he knew why. The ax was long and lethal, with a banded haft, a heavy head, and a wicked spike on its back, but it was a two-handed weapon. The Longinch would need to trust in his armor to protect him. I need to make him rue that choice.

His own shield was on his left arm, the shield Tanselle had painted with his elm and falling star. A child's rhyme echoed in his head. Oak and iron, guard me well, or else I'm dead, and doomed to hell. He slid his longsword from its scabbard. The weight of it felt good in his hands.

He put his heels into Thunder's flanks and walked the big destrier down into the water. Across the stream, Ser Lucas did the same. Dunk pressed right, so as to present the Longinch with his left side, protected by his shield. That was not something Ser Lucas was willing to concede him. He turned his courser quickly, and they came together in a tumult of gray steel and green spray. Ser Lucas struck with his poleax. Dunk had to twist in the saddle to catch it on his shield. The force of it shot down his arm and jarred his teeth together. He swung his sword in answer, a sideways cut that took the other knight beneath his upraised arm. Steel screamed on steel, and it was on.

The Longinch spurred his courser in a circle, trying to get around to Dunk's unprotected side, but Thunder wheeled to meet him, snapping at the other horse. Ser Lucas delivered one crashing blow after another, standing in his stirrups to get all his weight and strength behind the axhead. Dunk shifted his shield to catch each blow as it came. Half crouched beneath its oak, he hacked at Inchfield's arms and side and legs, but his plate turned every stroke. Around they went, and around again, the water lapping at their legs. The Longinch attacked, and Dunk defended, watching for a weakness.

Finally he saw it. Every time Ser Lucas lifted his ax for another blow, a gap appeared beneath his arm.

There was mail and leather there, and padding underneath, but no steel plate. Dunk kept his shield up, trying to time his attack. Soon. Soon. The ax crashed down, wrenched free, came up. Now! He slammed his spurs into Thunder, driving him closer, and thrust with his longsword, to drive his point through the opening.

But the gap vanished as quick as it had appeared. His swordpoint scraped a rondel, and Dunk, overextended, almost lost his seat. The ax descended with a crash, slanting off the iron rim of Dunk's shield, crunching against the side of his helm, and striking Thunder a glancing blow along the neck.

The destrier screamed and reared up on two legs, his eye rolling white in pain as the sharp coppery smell of blood filled the air. He lashed out with his iron hooves just as the Longinch was moving in. One caught Ser Lucas in the face, the other on a shoulder. Then the heavy warhorse came down atop his courser.

It all happened in a heartbeat. The two horses went down in a tangle, kicking and biting at each other, churning up the water and the mud below. Dunk tried to throw himself from the saddle, but one foot tangled in a stirrup. He fell face first, sucking down one desperate gulp of air before the stream came rushing into the helm through the eyeslit. His foot was still caught up, and he felt a savage yank as Thunder's struggles almost pulled his leg out of its socket. Just as quickly he was free, turning, sinking.

For a moment he flailed helplessly in the water. The world was blue and green and brown.

The weight of his armor pulled him down until his shoulder bumped the streambed. If that is down the other way is up. Dunk's steel-clad hands fumbled at the stones and sands, and somehow he gathered his legs up under him and stood. He was reeling, dripping mud, with water pouring from the breath holes in his dinted helm, but he was standing. He sucked down air.

His battered shield still clung to his left arm, but his scabbard was empty and his sword was gone. There was blood inside his helm as well as water. When he tried to shift his weight, his ankle sent a lance of pain right up his leg. Both horses had struggled back to their feet, he saw. He turned his head, squinting one-eyed through a veil of blood, searching for his foe. Gone, he thought, he's drowned, or Thunder crushed his skull in.

Ser Lucas burst up out of the water right in front of him, sword in hand. He struck Dunk's neck a savage blow, and only the thickness of his gorget kept his head upon his shoulders. He had no blade to answer with, only his shield. He gave ground, and the Longinch came after, screaming and slashing. Dunk's upraised arm took a numbing blow above the elbow. A cut to his hip made him grunt in pain. As he backed away, a rock turned beneath his foot, and he went down to one knee, chest-high in the water.

He got his shield up, but this time Ser Lucas struck so hard he split the thick oak right down the middle, and drove the remnants back into Dunk's face. His ears were ringing and his mouth was full of blood, but somewhere far away he heard Egg screaming. "Get him, ser, get him, get him, he's right there! "

Dunk dived forward. Ser Lucas had wrenched his sword free for another cut. Dunk slammed into him waist-high and knocked him off his feet. The stream swallowed both of them again, but this time Dunk was ready. He kept one arm around the Longinch and forced him to the bottom. Bubbles came streaming out from behind Inchfield's battered, twisted visor, but still he fought. He found a rock at the bottom of the stream and began hammering at Dunk's head and hands. Dunk fumbled at his swordbelt.

Have I lost the dagger too? he wondered. No, there it was. His hand closed around the hilt and he wrenched it free, and drove it slowly through the churning water, through the iron rings and boiled leather beneath the arm of Lucas the Longinch, turning it as he pushed. Ser Lucas jerked and twisted, and the strength left him. Dunk shoved away and floated. His chest was on fire. A fish flashed past his face, long and white and slender. What's that? he wondered. What's that? What's that?

He woke in the wrong castle.

When his eyes opened, he did not know where he was. It was blessedly cool. The taste of blood was in his mouth and he had a cloth across his eyes, a heavy cloth fragrant with some unguent. It smelled of cloves, he thought.

Dunk groped at his face, pulled the cloth away. Above him torchlight played against a high ceiling.

Ravens were walking on the rafters overhead, peering down with small black eyes and quork ing at him. I am not blind, at least. He was in a maester's tower. The walls were lined with racks of herbs and potions in earthen jars and vessels of green glass. A long trestle table nearby was covered with parchments, books, and queer bronze instruments, all spattered with droppings from the ravens in the rafters. He could hear them muttering at one another.

He tried to sit. It proved a bad mistake. His head swam, and his left leg screamed in agony when he put the slightest weight upon it. His ankle was wrapped in linen, he saw, and there were linen strips around his chest and shoulders, too.

"Be still." A face appeared above him, young and pinched, with dark brown eyes on either side of a hooked nose. Dunk knew that face. The man who owned it was all in gray, with a chain collar hanging loose about his neck, a maester's chain of many metals. Dunk grabbed him by the wrist. "Where? . . ."

"Coldmoat," said the maester. "You were too badly injured to return to Standfast, so Lady Rohanne commanded us to bring you here. Drink this." He raised a cup of . . . something . . . to Dunk's lips. The potion had a bitter taste, like vinegar, but at least it washed away the taste of blood.

Dunk made himself drink it all. Afterward he flexed the fingers of his sword hand, and then the other. At least my hands still work, and my arms. "What . . . what did I hurt?"

"What not?" The maester snorted. "A broken ankle, a sprained knee, a broken collarbone, bruising . . . your upper torso is largely green and yellow and your right arm is a purply black. I thought your skull was cracked as well, but it appears not. There is that gash in your face, ser. You will have a scar, I fear. Oh, and you had drowned by the time we pulled you from the water."

"Drowned?" said Dunk.

"I never suspected that one man could swallow so much water, not even a man as large as you, ser.

Count yourself fortunate that I am ironborn. The priests of the Drowned God know how to drown a man and bring him back, and I have made a study of their beliefs and customs."

I drowned. Dunk tried to sit again, but the strength was not in him. I drowned in water that did not even come up to my neck. He laughed, then groaned in pain. "Ser Lucas?"

"Dead. Did you doubt it?"

No. Dunk doubted many things, but not that. He remembered how the strength had gone out of the Longinch's limbs, all at once. "Egg," he got out. "I want Egg."

"Hunger is a good sign," the maester said, "but it is sleep you need just now, not food."

Dunk shook his head, and regretted it at once. "Egg is my squire . . ."

"Is he? A brave lad, and stronger than he looks. He was the one to pull you from the stream. He helped us get that armor off you, too, and rode with you in the wayn when we brought you here. He would not sleep himself, but sat by your side with your sword across his lap, in case someone tried to do you harm.

He even suspected me , and insisted that I taste anything I meant to feed you. A queer child, but devoted."

"Where is he?"

"Ser Eustace asked the boy to attend him at the wedding feast. There was no one else on his side. It would have been discourteous for him to refuse."

"Wedding feast?" Dunk did not understand.

"You would not know, of course. Coldmoat and Standfast were reconciled after your battle. Lady Rohanne begged leave of old Ser Eustace to cross his land and visit Addam's grave, and he granted her that right. She knelt before the blackberries and began to weep, and he was so moved that he went to comfort her. They spent the whole night talking of young Addam and my lady's noble father. Lord Wyman and Ser Eustace were fast friends, until the Blackfyre Rebellion. His lordship and my lady were wed this morning, by our good Septon Sefton. Eustace Osgrey is the lord of Coldmoat, and his chequy lion flies beside the Webber spider on every tower and wall."

Dunk's world was spinning slowly all around him. That potion. He's put me back to sleep. He closed his eyes, and let all the pain drain out of him. He could hear the ravens quork ing and screaming at each other, and the sound of his own breath, and something else as well . . . a softer sound, steady, heavy, somehow soothing. "What's that?" he murmured sleepily. "That sound? . . ."

"That?" The maester listened. "That's just rain."

He did not see her till the day they took their leave.

"This is folly, ser," Septon Sefton complained, as Dunk limped heavily across the yard, swinging his splinted foot and leaning on a crutch. "Maester Cerrick says you are not half healed as yet, and this rain . . . you're like to catch a chill, if you do not drown again. At least wait for the rain to stop."

"That may be years." Dunk was grateful to the fat septon, who had visited him near every day . . . to pray for him, ostensibly, though more time seemed to be taken up with tales and gossip. He would miss his loose and lively tongue and cheerful company, but that changed nothing. "I need to go."

The rain was lashing down around them, a thousand cold gray whips upon his back. His cloak was already sodden. It was the white wool cloak Ser Eustace had given him, with the green-and-gold-checkered border. The old knight had pressed it on him once again, as a parting gift.

"For your courage and leal service, ser," he said. The brooch that pinned the cloak at his shoulder was a gift as well; an ivory spider brooch with silver legs. Clusters of crushed garnets made spots upon its back.

"I hope this is not some mad quest to hunt down Bennis," Septon Sefton said. "You are so bruised and battered that I would fear for you, if that one found you in such a state."

Bennis, Dunk thought bitterly, bloody Bennis . While Dunk had been making his stand at the stream, Bennis had tied up Sam Stoops and his wife, ransacked Standfast from top to bottom, and made off with every item of value he could find, from candles, clothes, and weaponry to Osgrey's old silver cup and a small cache of coin the old man had hidden in his solar behind a mildewed tapestry. One day Dunk hoped to meet Ser Bennis of the Brown Shield again, and when he did . . . "Bennis will keep."

"Where will you go?" The septon was panting heavily. Even with Dunk on a crutch, he was too fat to match his pace.

"Fair Isle. Harrenhal. The Trident. There are hedges everywhere." He shrugged. "I've always wanted to see the Wall."

"The Wall?" The septon jerked to a stop. "I despair of you, Ser Duncan!" he shouted, standing in the mud with outspread hands as the rain came down around him. "Pray, ser, pray for the Crone to light your way!" Dunk kept walking.

She was waiting for him inside the stables, standing by the yellow bales of hay in a gown as green as summer. "Ser Duncan," she said when he came pushing through the door. Her red braid hung down in front, the end of it brushing against her thighs. "It is good to see you on your feet."

You never saw me on my back, he thought. "M'lady. What brings you to the stables. It's a wet day for a ride."

"I might say the same to you."

"Egg told you?" I owe him another clout in the ear.

"Be glad he did, or I would have sent men after you to drag you back. It was cruel of you to try and steal away without so much as a farewell."

She had never come to see him while he was in Maester Cerrick's care, not once. "That green becomes you well, m'lady," he said. "It brings out the color of your eyes." He shifted his weight awkwardly on the crutch. "I'm here for my horse."

"You do not need to go. There is a place for you here, when you're recovered. Captain of my guards.

And Egg can join my other squires. No one need ever know who he is."

"Thank you, m'lady, but no." Thunder was in a stall a dozen places down. Dunk hobbled toward him.

"Please reconsider, ser. These are perilous times, even for dragons and their friends. Stay until you've healed." She walked along beside him. "It would please Lord Eustace, too. He is very fond of you."

"Very fond," Dunk agreed. "If his daughter weren't dead, he'd want me to marry her. Then you could be my lady mother. I never had a mother, much less a lady mother."

For half a heartbeat Lady Rohanne looked as though she was going to slap him again. Maybe she'll just kick my crutch away.

"You are angry with me, ser," she said instead. "You must let me make amends."

"Well," he said, "you could help me saddle Thunder."

"I had something else in mind." She reached out her hand for his, a freckled hand, her fingers strong and slender. I'll bet she's freckled all over. "How well do you know horses?"

"I ride one."

"An old destrier bred for battle, slow-footed and ill-tempered. Not a horse to ride from place to place."

"If I need to get from place to place, it's him or these." Dunk pointed at his feet.

"You have large feet," she observed. "Large hands as well. I think you must be large all over. Too large for most palfreys. They'd look like ponies with you perched upon their backs. Still, a swifter mount would serve you well. A big courser, with some Dornish sand steed for endurance." She pointed to the stall across from Thunder's. "A horse like her."

She was a blood bay with a bright eye and a long fiery mane. Lady Rohanne took a carrot from her sleeves and stroked her head as she took it. "The carrot, not the fingers," she told the horse, before she turned again to Dunk. "I call her Flame, but you may name her as you please. Call her Amends, if you like."

For a moment he was speechless. He leaned on the crutch and looked at the blood bay with new eyes.

She was magnificent. A better mount than any the old man had ever owned. You had only to look at those long, clean limbs to see how swift she'd be.

"I bred her for beauty, and for speed."

He turned back to Thunder. "I cannot take her."

"Why not?"

"She is too good a horse for me. Just look at her."

A flush crept up Rohanne's face. She clutched her braid, twisting it between her fingers. "I had to marry, you know that. My father's will . . . oh, don't be such a fool."

"What else should I be? I'm thick as a castle wall and bastard born as well."

"Take the horse. I refuse to let you go without something to remember me by."

"I will remember you, m'lady. Have no fear of that."

"Take her!"

Dunk grabbed her braid and pulled her face to his. It was awkward with the crutch and the difference in their heights. He almost fell before he got his lips on hers. He kissed her hard. One of her hands went around his neck, and one around his back. He learned more about kissing in a moment than he had ever known from watching. But when they finally broke apart, he drew his dagger. "I know what I want to remember you by, m'lady."

Egg was waiting for him at the gatehouse, mounted on a handsome new sorrel palfrey and holding Maester's lead. When Dunk trotted up to them on Thunder, the boy looked surprised. "She said she wanted to give you a new horse, ser."

"Even highborn ladies don't get all they want," Dunk said, as they rode out across the drawbridge. "It wasn't a horse I wanted." The moat was so high it was threatening to overflow its banks. "I took something else to remember her by instead. A lock of that red hair." He reached under his cloak, brought out the braid, and smiled.

In the iron cage at the crossroads, the corpses still embraced. They looked lonely, forlorn. Even the flies had abandoned them, and the crows as well. Only some scraps of skin and hair remained upon the dead men's bones.

Dunk halted, frowning. His ankle was hurting from the ride, but it made no matter. Pain was as much a part of knighthood as were swords and shields. "Which way is south?" he asked Egg. It was hard to know, when the world was all rain and mud and the sky was gray as a granite wall.

"That's south, ser." Egg pointed. "That's north."

"Summerhall is south. Your father."

"The Wall is north."

Dunk looked at him. "That's a long way to ride."

"I have a new horse, ser."

"So you do." Dunk had to smile. "And why would you want to see the Wall?"

"Well," said Egg. "I hear it's tall."

file.content:
THK / the bald stableboy "Egg" / cross-identity / Egg revealed in-text as Prince Aegon Targaryen, Maekar's youngest son; classic D&E concealed-identity reveal — "I cut it off, brother. I didn't want to look like you."
THK / the dreaming lordling at the inn / cross-identity / The drunk lordling Dunk meets is Prince Daeron, Maekar's heir; "Daeron the Drunken" concealed under common appearance.
THK / a black knight in white scale armor / cross-identity / Baelor fights the trial in Valarr's borrowed armor and is briefly mistaken for his own son before lifting his visor.
THK / "We were the masters of dragons once, we Targaryens" / targaryen-history / Targaryen dragon-mastery and the line's near-extinction in Daeron II's father's day; "Daeron II and his sons had left it secure for all time."
THK / "they'd seen the last dragon there the year before it died" / targaryen-history / The last dragon: a stunted green female, eggs never hatched; some say Aegon the Unlucky/Dragonbane poisoned her after a dragon devoured his mother.
THK / "The summers have been shorter since the last dragon died" / prophecy / Seasonal lore tying the death of dragons to shortened summers and crueler winters.
THK / "I dreamed of you and a dead dragon" / prophecy / Daeron's true dream of Dunk and a dead dragon — a Targaryen prophetic-dreaming hook.
THK / "A trial of seven... It came across the narrow sea with the Andals and their seven gods" / causal-spine / Aerion demands a trial of seven → Dunk must field seven champions → Baelor joins → Baelor dies in the melee.
THK / "it was my mace that dealt the fatal blow" / causal-spine / Dunk strikes Aerion to protect Tanselle → Dunk is tried → trial of seven → Maekar's mace kills Baelor.
THK / lamb roasted with a crust of herbs, duck cooked with cherries and lemons / food / Loadbearing inn-meal description marking Dunk's first taste of "what it means to be a knight."
THK / a goat basted with honey and herbs / hospitality / Fossoway pavilion hospitality Dunk is uneasy accepting; later Baelor's sweet Dornish red in the solar.
THK / "the maesters shaved it off" / description / Egg's shaved head — the concrete detail concealing dragon-bright silver-gold hair; the mystery-engine object of the novella.
THK / the elm tree on a sunset field with a shooting star / description / Dunk's new sigil, painted by Tanselle, replacing Ser Arlan's winged chalice; "all painted up like death," a falling star.
THK / "It's short for Aegon. My brother Aemon named me Egg" / cross-identity / Stableboy "Egg" revealed in-text as Prince Aegon Targaryen, Maekar's youngest son
THK / "Ser Duncan the Tall" / cross-identity / Flea Bottom orphan "Dunk" self-invents a knightly name and house-less identity
THK / "I did not think to enter the lists at Ashford... so I brought no armor. My son was good enough to lend me his" / cross-identity / Prince Baelor fights the trial disguised in son Valarr's black dragon armor
THK / "Daeron the Drunken" / cross-identity / The drunk inn lordling revealed as Prince Daeron, Maekar's heir
THK / "My dreams are not like yours, Ser Duncan. Mine are true... I dreamed of you and a dead dragon" / prophecy / Prince Daeron's prophetic dream of Dunk and a dead dragon
THK / "A falling star brings luck to him who sees it" / prophecy / falling-star luck omen seeded the night before the tourney
THK / "Good King Daeron had four grown sons, three with sons of their own" / targaryen-history / Daeron II succession map: Baelor, Aerys, Rhaegel, Maekar and their sons
THK / "the last dragon... None of her eggs had ever hatched. Some say King Aegon poisoned her" / targaryen-history / death of the last dragon; Aegon III the Dragonbane poisoning rumor; summers shortened since
THK / "heir apparent to the Iron Throne" / targaryen-history / Baelor Breakspear, Hand and heir, dies at Ashford; succession shifts toward Valarr
THK / "I have sent Aerion to Lys" / targaryen-history / Aerion Brightflame banished to the Free Cities by Maekar
THK / "My brother Aemon... is off at the Citadel now, learning to be a maester" / targaryen-history / Prince Aemon given to the Citadel
THK / "good lamb, roasted with a crust of herbs, and some ducks my son shot down" / food / inn meal: herbed lamb, duck with cherries and lemons, buttered pease, oaten bread
THK / "a goat basted with honey and herbs" / hospitality / Fossoway pavilion hospitality (Arbor wine + goat) Dunk feels he cannot return
THK / "Pour Ser Duncan a cup of the sweet Dornish red" / hospitality / Baelor's solar hospitality before delivering the trial choice
THK / "the fierce three-headed dragon of House Targaryen seemed to spread its wings" / description / royal banner arrival set-piece; full Ashford heraldry roll-call
THK / Aerion maims Tanselle the puppeteer / causal-spine / Aerion breaks Tanselle's finger over the slain-dragon puppet -> Dunk attacks Aerion
THK / Dunk strikes and kicks Prince Aerion / causal-spine / Dunk beats Aerion -> Dunk arrested for striking the blood of the dragon
THK / Aerion demands a trial of seven / causal-spine / trial of seven called -> Dunk must find six champions
THK / no knight will stand for Dunk / causal-spine / Dunk's plea unanswered -> Baelor secretly takes his side as the seventh
THK / the trial of seven is fought / causal-spine / melee -> Maekar's mace accidentally kills his brother Baelor
THK / Baelor dies so a hedge knight might live / causal-spine / Baelor's death -> Maekar gives Egg to Dunk as squire
THK / "A hedge knight is the truest kind of knight" / foreshadow-hook / Ser Arlan's creed on hedge knighthood and protecting the weak, recalled in the cell
TSS / "a dragon is a different sort of beast" / cross-identity / Dunk reveals Egg's blood to Rohanne via Maekar's four-dragon signet hidden in the boot; the Widow stays her hand
TSS / "Brynden Rivers was the Hand's true name" / cross-identity / Lord Bloodraven named in-text as Brynden Rivers, half-Blackwood bastard of Aegon IV, now King Aerys's Hand
TSS / "the fourth and youngest son of Maekar" / cross-identity / Stableboy "Egg" is Aegon Targaryen, concealed under a shaved head and a hedge knight's squireship
TSS / "He had legitimized the lot upon his deathbed" / targaryen-history / Aegon IV the Unworthy legitimized the Great Bastards (Bloodraven, Bittersteel, Daemon Blackfyre) and gave Blackfyre to Daemon
TSS / "Thus perished the black dragon and his sons" / targaryen-history / Detailed Redgrass Field account: Daemon and twins Aegon/Aemon slain by Bloodraven's arrows; Bittersteel turns the rout
TSS / "His Grace King Daeron, sweet Matarys and bold Valarr, the Hand" / targaryen-history / Great Spring Sickness killed Daeron II, princes Valarr and Matarys, the High Septon; cut down four of ten in King's Landing
TSS / "He will not even bestir himself to sire an heir" / targaryen-history / King Aerys I heirless; Queen Aelinor remains a maid; Bloodraven rules with spells and spies; succession instability seeded
TSS / "Coldmoat was promised me by Daemon" / targaryen-history / Eustace Osgrey rose for the black dragon hoping to regain Osgrey castles; the rebel-loyalist framing of treason
TSS / "all bastards are born to betrayal" / prophecy / The old High Septon's pronouncement to Maekar that Daemon, Bittersteel, and Bloodraven would all prove traitors
TSS / "cares more for old scrolls and dusty prophecies" / prophecy / King Aerys's obsession with prophecy noted; a hook for Targaryen prophetic preoccupation
TSS / "we'd eat them in a bowl of cream" / food / Blackberries in cream — Addam Osgrey and Rohanne's loadbearing courtship detail tied to the grave in the blackberries
TSS / "This is an Arbor vintage, very fine, and the poison gives it a special piquance" / hospitality / Coldmoat wine offered under suspicion of the Red Widow's poisonings; Egg refuses his cup
TSS / "No bread and salt, not even shade and water" / hospitality / Ser Eustace explicitly denies guest right to Lady Rohanne's party at the stream boundary
TSS / "the maesters shaved it off" / description / Egg's shaved head and deep-purple Valyrian eyes — the concrete concealed-identity object of the novella
TSS / "a rampant lion made of checkered squares" / description / The chequy lion carved over Coldmoat's gate beneath the newer Webber spider — Osgrey lost-greatness motif
TSS / Bennis cuts the digger Wolmer at the dam / causal-spine / Bennis opens Wolmer's cheek → Lady Rohanne demands Bennis or blood → trial by battle
TSS / Wat's Wood burns in the night / causal-spine / The wood burns → Eustace confronts the Widow at the stream → trial by battle → Lucas Longinch dies
TSS / Dunk cuts his own cheek "A cheek for a cheek" / causal-spine / Dunk pays the blood price in his own blood → Rohanne demands a trial of her innocence over the fire
TSS / "His sons paid for his treason with their life's blood" / causal-spine / Eustace's rebellion → royal grant of the Chequy Water to the Webbers → drought-era water war between Standfast and Coldmoat
TSS / "I hear it's tall" / foreshadow-hook / Egg and Dunk turn north toward the Wall; Dunk's old notion of finding a tall man like himself at the Wall
THK / "the maesters shaved it off" / cross-identity / Stableboy "Egg" introduced with a shaved bald head and named only "Egg"; concealed-identity engine seeded, no reveal in this unit
THK / "Egg," he said. / cross-identity / The boy gives only the nickname "Egg" (Dunk: "His head does look like an egg"); true name withheld
THK / "Some say King Aegon poisoned her" / targaryen-history / The last dragon (stunted green female) died in King's Landing; Aegon III Dragonbane/the Unlucky blamed; summers shortened, winters lengthened since
THK / "he'd seen his uncle's beast devour his own mother" / targaryen-history / Aegon III feared dragons after a dragon devoured his mother; deep Targaryen dynastic-trauma hook
THK / "unhorsed by the Prince of Dragonstone in a tourney at Storm's End" / targaryen-history / Ser Arlan broke seven lances against the Prince of Dragonstone, "the finest knight in the Seven Kingdoms"
THK / "I dreamed of you. You stay away from me" / prophecy / Drunk lordling claims a dream of Dunk and warns him off; prophetic-dream hook tied to the tourney
THK / "a bright green streak that flashed across the black" / foreshadow-hook / A green falling star on the first night at Ashford; Dunk claims "the luck is mine alone"
THK / lamb roasted with a crust of herbs; duck cooked with cherries and lemons / food / Loadbearing inn meal contrasted with lords' capons and suckling pigs and Dunk's usual salt beef
THK / "some ducks my son shot down" / hospitality / Commercial inn hospitality; Dunk buys food and ale, declines a room to save coin
THK / "a winged chalice, silver on brown" / description / Ser Arlan of Pennytree's sigil on the oaken shield Dunk inherits and slings on his back to pass as a knight
THK / "Ser Duncan the Tall" / cross-identity / Dunk invents his knightly name on the page (from "Dunk," of Flea Bottom, no house)
THK / Ser Arlan dies of a road-chill / causal-spine / Ser Arlan dies -> Dunk inherits his sword, shield, and horses and resolves to enter the Ashford tourney
THK / Dunk commissions armor from Steely Pate on credit / causal-spine / Dunk pays only a two-silver deposit -> he must win a victory to raise the rest of the coin
THK / Egg rides to Ashford in a lamb-cart / causal-spine / Dunk refuses Egg at the inn -> Egg follows to the camp -> Dunk takes him on as squire
THK / "the fierce three-headed dragon of House Targaryen seemed to spread its wings, breathing scarlet fire" / targaryen-history / Targaryen royal party arrives at Ashford under the black three-headed-dragon banner with Kingsguard
THK / "Good King Daeron had four grown sons, three with sons of their own" / targaryen-history / Daeron II's line laid out; the dragonkings' succession "secure for all time"
THK / "The line of the dragonkings had almost died out during his father's day" / targaryen-history / Near-extinction of the Targaryen line a generation before Daeron II
THK / "their silver-gold hair and violet eyes set them apart from common men" / description / Targaryen Valyrian features named as identity marker — the concealed-identity key of the whole cycle
THK / "His eyes were a deep violet color" / cross-identity / Unnamed arrogant Targaryen princeling insults Dunk at the stables; silver-gold hair, violet eyes; identity withheld in this unit
THK / "blue eyes, very dark, almost purple" / cross-identity / Egg's eyes described as almost purple beneath his shaved bald head; Targaryen-feature hook not connected on the page
THK / "the third was older, well worn, and showed a different man... Dunk could not read the letters" / targaryen-history / An older unnamed king on a worn gold dragon; illiterate Dunk cannot read the name
THK / "Prince Valarr has the honor to be one of Lady Ashford's champions" / other / Valarr, eldest son of Baelor Breakspear, and two cousin-princes set to compete at Ashford
THK / "It would not be fitting for us to ride against those we are sworn to protect" / other / Kingsguard oath to the royal family stated; Ser Roland Crakehall and Ser Donnel of Duskendale attend
THK / lamb, duck, sausages, woodsmoke and bacon across the tourney field / food / Tourney-ground fare; winesellers and sausage makers doing brisk trade
THK / Plummer requires a knight to vouch for Dunk / causal-spine / Dunk enters his name as Ser Duncan the Tall → Plummer demands a voucher → Dunk must bring Ser Manfred Dondarrion on the morrow
THK / Dunk sells Sweetfoot to Henly for 750 silver stags / causal-spine / Dunk sells his palfrey → raises coin toward the armor debt owed to Steely Pate
THK / "He likes to batter men in the training yard, so they will be bruised and vulnerable later" / causal-spine / Ser Steffon batters cousin Raymun and baits Dunk → Dunk resolves to hide his skill and enter the lists only on the third day
THK / "I am called Ser Duncan the Tall" / cross-identity / Dunk (of Flea Bottom, no house) states his invented knightly name to Tanselle
THK / "I am speaking with Baelor Targaryen, Prince of Dragonstone, Hand of the King, and heir apparent" / targaryen-history / Baelor Breakspear revealed on-page; succession line to the Iron Throne stated
THK / "He could only be Prince Maekar, the youngest of King Daeron's four sons" / targaryen-history / Daeron II's four sons enumerated (Baelor, Aerys, Rhaegel, Maekar); Maekar identified by his pox-scarred silver beard
THK / "Prince Aerys was bookish and Prince Rhaegel mad, meek, and sickly" / targaryen-history / Characterization of Daeron II's middle sons who did not attend
THK / "Two of his sons have gone astray on their way here" / causal-spine / Maekar's sons Daeron and Aegon missing -> Ser Roland sent to find them
THK / "he'd sooner ride a whore than a horse" / targaryen-history / Maekar-Baelor quarrel over Prince Daeron's fitness for the lists; Daeron is eighteen
THK / "Ser Damon Lannister! The Grey Lion! He's Lord of Casterly Rock now" / cross-identity / Dunk recalls the Grey Lion's true name to pass Baelor's test of his claim
THK / "he took for his arms a forked purple lightning bolt, on a black field powdered with stars" / description / Dondarrion sigil origin tale (first Lord Dondarrion, the Storm King, the Dornish Marches)
THK / "By law, only a trueborn son is entitled to inherit a knight's arms" / causal-spine / Baelor bars Dunk from Arlan's winged-chalice arms -> Dunk commissions a new device (sunset field, elm, shooting star) from Tanselle
THK / "the soul of chivalry, and that one day the Seven Kingdoms would be safe in your hands" / targaryen-history / Arlan's praise of Baelor Breakspear as future king, recalled by Dunk to Baelor's face
THK / "all men are fools, and all men are knights, where women are concerned" / description / Florian-and-Jonquil puppet play performed at Ashford; Florian in motley armor, a painted dragon and giant
THK / Ser Manfred Dondarrion refuses to vouch for Dunk / causal-spine / Manfred's refusal -> Dunk goes to the Great Hall seeking Plummer -> overhears the princes -> Baelor vouches
THK / "fresh bread... a bit of cheese... good cheese at one of the stalls" / food / Tourney-market fare Dunk plans to buy; winesellers and sausage makers doing brisk trade
THK / "I cut it off, brother. I didn't want to look like you." / cross-identity / Egg the squire/stableboy revealed in-text as Prince Aerion's brother and Maekar's son; shaved head conceals Targaryen coloring
THK / "answer to my father. And my uncle as well" / cross-identity / Egg invokes his father (Maekar) and uncle (Baelor, present at Ashford) to command Aerion's men-at-arms; princely identity reveal
THK / "the king's fourth son... Daeron is a sot, Aerion is vain and cruel, the third son... a maester, and the youngest" / targaryen-history / Full rundown of Maekar's four sons and his three brothers (Baelor, Aerys, Rhaegel); succession-order context
THK / "grandson to Daeron the Good, the Second of His Name" / targaryen-history / Herald's recitation places Aerion/Maekar in the Daeron II line
THK / "his blood is the blood of Aegon the Conqueror and the Young Dragon and Prince Aemon the Dragonknight" / targaryen-history / Valarr's bloodline listed; Valarr second in line to the Iron Throne
THK / "the youngest boy was with him... They left Summerhall together but never reached Ashford" / foreshadow-hook / Maekar's missing sons and the "wild tale of robber knights"; Maekar off searching with Roland Crakehall
THK / "goat with honey and herbs over a small cookfire" / food / Fossoway pavilion hospitality; Arbor-vintage wine served to Dunk
THK / "It's an Arbor vintage, too. How can you refuse me?" / hospitality / Raymun Fossoway presses hospitality on Dunk in Ser Steffon's gold pavilion; Dunk uneasy he cannot return it
THK / "break their fast on goose eggs, fried bread, and bacon" / food / Tourney-morning breakfast; Dunk has no appetite before the first day
THK / "Aerion's lance took the animal just above the armor... exploded out of the back of his neck" / causal-spine / Aerion deliberately kills Ser Humfrey Hardyng's horse -> Humfrey's leg broken in two places -> Humfrey withdraws, four champions remain
THK / "he's hurting her. The puppet girl" / causal-spine / Aerion assaults Tanselle -> Dunk beats Aerion -> Dunk seized by Aerion's men -> Egg reveals identity -> men release Dunk
THK / "a puppet show about Nymeria, the warrior queen with the ten thousand ships" / description / Merchants'-row puppet shows (Nymeria, Florian and Jonquil, Symeon Star-Eyes, a fighting dragon puppet) staged by Tanselle
THK / "It's short for Aegon. My brother Aemon named me Egg." / cross-identity / Squire "Egg" reveals true name Aegon; brothers Aemon (at the Citadel), Daeron (eldest); uncle Prince Baelor — full Targaryen identity naming
THK / "It was him shaved my head... mine is like Aerion's and my father's" / cross-identity / Concealment mechanism explained: Daeron shaved Egg's head to hide silver-gold Targaryen hair and purple eyes ("the blood of the dragon")
THK / "Like Aegon the Dragon. How many Aegons have been king? Four." / targaryen-history / Four King Aegons counted; Egg named for Aegon the Conqueror
THK / "I am the King's Hand and the heir to the throne" / targaryen-history / Baelor Breakspear stated as Hand of the King and heir; his brother Maekar Prince of Summerhall; the King (Daeron II) hundreds of leagues away

264	| `RESCUES` | Dramatic single-moment extraction from danger — rescuer saves the target from death, capture, or peril. Distinct from `PROTECTS` (steady-state guardianship) and `HEALS` (medical restoration). Covers Beric rescuing BWB members, Davos rescued by Salladhor, Sam & Gilly rescued by the Mysterious Rider, Jon rescuing Ghost from wildlings. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Rescuer → Rescued-person | corpus |
265	| `TORTURES` | The act of inflicting deliberate physical pain — flaying, racking, burning, or other sustained bodily torment. Distinct from `ATTACKS` (generic physical violence; not necessarily sustained or systematic), `ASSAULTS` (sexual violence specifically), `EXECUTES` (formal death sentence), and the `REVEALS_TO=under_torture` qualifier (which captures the informational *effect* of torture, not the act itself). Major narrative thread: Bolton flaying tradition, Mountain at Harrenhal, Qyburn's "studies", the Bloody Mummers, Black Cells. Without this edge, "who has Ramsay tortured?" cannot be graph-answered. Tier-3 (no qualifier; method is sub-typology the narrative captures). | Torturer → Tortured-person | corpus |
266	| `AGENT_IN` | Acts as the agent/executor of an event — the participant who actually performed the act. Source is the executor; target is the event node. Reification role edge: use when attaching a character or house to a multi-party event hub instead of (or in addition to) a dyadic binary edge. See `VICTIM_IN` (patient), `COMMANDS_IN` (orderer who did not personally execute), `WIELDED_IN` (instrument). | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
267	| `VICTIM_IN` | Receives the action of an event as the victim or patient — the participant on whom the act was performed. Source is the recipient; target is the event node. Reification role edge: complement to `AGENT_IN` on the same event hub. | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
268	| `WITNESS_IN` | The **observer/perceiver** slot on an event hub — a character whose *load-bearing perception* of a charged event (violence, death, a secret, an atrocity) is itself worth a graph edge, distinct from acting (`AGENT_IN`), suffering (`VICTIM_IN`), ordering (`COMMANDS_IN`), or combatting (`FIGHTS_IN`). **Default to an EDGE** onto the existing event node (the Q2 three-lens consensus, S117): reify the witnessing as its own `event.*` NODE *only* when the act of seeing owns an outgoing causal edge — i.e. the seeing `CAUSES`/`TRIGGERS`/`MOTIVATES` a downstream event (e.g. `bran-witnesses-jaime-and-cersei`, whose seeing causes his fall) — or the witnessed thing has no node of its own. Mechanical test: *does the seeing need an outgoing edge? yes → node; no → edge.* Promotion path: start as an edge, promote to a node later only if a causal edge actually gets drawn. **Text-anchor gate:** emit only when the prose shows the character actually SEES it — present-but-shielded does NOT qualify (Yoren forces Arya's eyes shut at Ned's execution → Arya is NOT a witness; Sansa, who "could not turn her head," IS). **Boundary vs `ATTENDS`:** `ATTENDS` = voluntary audience at a *staged social/ceremonial gathering* (feast, wedding, tourney-as-a-whole, court, coronation); `WITNESS_IN` = perception of a charged/violent/secret incident. Same person can `ATTENDS` the Tourney of the Hand AND `WITNESS_IN` the Loras-vs-Gregor bout within it (different targets). Tier-1 when verbatim-cited. Added Session 117 (2026-06-21); first instances `sansa-stark WITNESS_IN execution-of-eddard-stark`, `arianne-martell WITNESS_IN myrcella-is-maimed-by-darkstar`, `hoster-tully WITNESS_IN battle-of-the-camps`. | Person → Event (`event.*`) | — |
269	
270	> **Reification role vocabulary note:** `WIELDED_IN` (already in the Possession & Artifacts section, artifact→event) serves the **instrument** role for reified events — no `INSTRUMENT_IN` type is needed. `COMMANDS_IN` (below) serves the **orderer/instigator** role. Together with `AGENT_IN` and `VICTIM_IN`, these four cover the standard **action/instrument** participant slots (agent, patient, orderer, instrument) on an event hub. The **perceiver/observer** slot is a fifth role, `WITNESS_IN` (S117) — epistemic, not action (the perceiver acts on nothing and receives nothing); voluntary audience at a *staged* gathering is `ATTENDS`, not `WITNESS_IN`.

Stage 4 wiki-prose classification ran an 82.3% fallback rate on KNOWS emits — the semantic boundary ("knows of" vs "met once" vs "heard rumor of") is too blurry for prose-derived classification to enforce. Character-knowledge relationships will be derived from a future Pass-1-based chapter co-occurrence + Information Revealed pass. Existing KNOWS edges in the graph (363 Haiku-emit + 21 Sonnet-control-arm) are preserved as historical record; downstream consumers filter deprecated types on read.

# TSS — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TSS
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Coldmoat (and the stream where the trial by battle is fought)
- **location_secondary:** Standfast (mentioned), Goldengrove (mentioned), Summerhall (mentioned), the Wall / Fair Isle / Harrenhal / the Trident (named as destinations), the crossroads with the iron cage
- **approximate_timeline:** The trial by battle at the stream, then Dunk's several-day recovery at Coldmoat, the wedding "this morning," and the departure on a later rainy day
- **time_markers:** "It's too hot to die"; "It had been so long" (since clouds/rain); the wedding "this morning"; the septon visited "near every day"; "the day they took their leave"; drought giving way to steady rain
- **chapter_summary:** Dunk fights Ser Lucas Inchfield in a stream as champion for Ser Eustace Osgrey in a trial by battle, and after both horses go down and Dunk himself briefly drowns, he kills the Longinch with his dagger underwater. He wakes at Coldmoat, revived by the ironborn maester Cerrick, and learns that Ser Eustace and Lady Rohanne reconciled at her weeping over Addam's grave and were wed that morning, making Eustace lord of Coldmoat. As Dunk prepares to leave despite his injuries, Lady Rohanne offers him a captaincy of her guards, a place for Egg, and a fine blood-bay mare, all of which he refuses; he kisses her and cuts off her red braid as a keepsake. Dunk and Egg ride out, and at the crossroads they choose to turn north toward the Wall rather than south to Summerhall.
- **unit_part:** 8 of 8

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Hot and dry before the fight; smoke from the burned wood mostly blown away; sky dark with clouds. During recovery and departure, steady heavy rain ("a thousand cold gray whips upon his back"); the moat threatening to overflow its banks.
- **Season indicators:** Drought breaking into rain; summer heat ("It's too hot to die"; "a gown as green as summer")
- **Time of day:** Battle by day under a darkening sky; Dunk wakes to torchlight; departure in daylight rain
- **Lighting:** Torchlight against the maester's tower ceiling; the underwater world "blue and green and brown"; the sky "gray as a granite wall"
- **Sounds:** Ravens quorking and muttering in the rafters; the sound of rain (mistaken by Dunk for something else); the ringing in Dunk's ears; Egg screaming during the fight
- **Smells:** The sharp coppery smell of blood; the cloth over Dunk's eyes fragrant with unguent smelling of cloves
- **Notable sensory details:** The maester's tower "blessedly cool"; the bitter vinegar taste of the potion washing away the taste of blood; water pouring from the breath holes of Dunk's dinted helm

## Events & Actions
1. **Septon Sefton prays and pleads before the trial** — Sefton says a prayer to the Father, Warrior, and Mother, then begs Ser Eustace to withdraw the accusation and Lady Rohanne to confess, and proposes taking the matter to Lord Rowan at Goldengrove instead.

4. **The trial by battle** — Dunk and Ser Lucas fight mounted in the stream; Lucas wields a two-handed poleax, Dunk his sword and shield. Dunk's thrust at a gap under Lucas's arm fails; Lucas's ax strikes Dunk's helm and wounds Thunder's neck.

5. **Thunder strikes Ser Lucas and the horses fall** — Wounded, Thunder rears and lashes out, one hoof catching Lucas in the face and another on the shoulder, then comes down atop the courser; both horses go down in a tangle.

6. **Dunk drowns and rises** — Dunk falls face-first, one foot caught in a stirrup, and the stream floods his helm; his leg nearly pulled from its socket by Thunder's struggles, he is briefly submerged, then gathers his legs and stands, having lost his sword.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The stream
   - Outcome: Dunk survives but loses his sword and is injured
7. **Dunk kills Ser Lucas** — Lucas bursts up sword in hand and drives Dunk down, splitting his shield; Egg screams for Dunk to fight; Dunk tackles Lucas underwater, holds him down, draws his dagger, and drives it through the mail and leather beneath Lucas's arm, turning it until the strength leaves him.

8. **Egg pulls Dunk from the stream and guards him** — Egg (himself drowned by the water's account, revived) pulls the drowned Dunk from the stream, helps remove his armor, rides with him in the wayn to Coldmoat, and sits by his side with the sword across his lap, making the maester taste Dunk's food.
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Location: The stream / Coldmoat
   - Outcome: Dunk brought to Coldmoat and guarded
9. **Maester Cerrick revives and treats Dunk** — The ironborn maester, drawing on Drowned God priests' methods, brings the drowned Dunk back and treats his broken ankle, sprained knee, broken collarbone, bruising, and facial gash.
   - Agent: Maester Cerrick
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Location: Coldmoat maester's tower
   - Outcome: Dunk revived and healing
10. **Ser Eustace and Lady Rohanne reconcile and wed** — After the battle, Rohanne asks leave to cross Eustace's land to visit Addam's grave, weeps before the blackberries, and Eustace comforts her; they talk all night of Addam and her father, and are wed that morning by Septon Sefton, making Eustace lord of Coldmoat.

    - Outcome: Dunk refuses
12. **Dunk kisses Rohanne and cuts off her braid** — Dunk grabs Rohanne's braid, pulls her to him, kisses her, then draws his dagger and cuts off the braid as the keepsake he chooses instead of the horse.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: Lady Rohanne
    - Instrument: Dagger
    - Location: Coldmoat stables

## Events & Actions
1. **Septon Sefton prays and pleads before the trial** — Sefton says a prayer to the Father, Warrior, and Mother, then begs Ser Eustace to withdraw the accusation and Lady Rohanne to confess, and proposes taking the matter to Lord Rowan at Goldengrove instead.
   - Agent: Septon Sefton
   - Patient: Ser Eustace, Lady Rohanne
   - Location: The stream
   - Outcome: Both refuse; the trial proceeds
2. **Ser Lucas asserts his claim to marry Rohanne** — Lucas tells Rohanne, "You will marry me when this mummer's farce is done. As your lord father wished"; she answers that her father never knew him as she does.
   - Agent: Ser Lucas Inchfield
   - Patient: Lady Rohanne
   - Location: The stream
3. **Dunk returns the signet to Egg** — Dunk kneels, puts the signet (Maekar's four three-headed dragons) back in Egg's hand, and instructs him, if Dunk dies, to go to the nearest of his father's friends to be taken back to Summerhall.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Egg
   - Instrument: Maekar's signet
   - Location: The stream
4. **The trial by battle** — Dunk and Ser Lucas fight mounted in the stream; Lucas wields a two-handed poleax, Dunk his sword and shield. Dunk's thrust at a gap under Lucas's arm fails; Lucas's ax strikes Dunk's helm and wounds Thunder's neck.
   - Agent: Dunk / Ser Lucas
   - Patient: Ser Lucas / Dunk
   - Instrument: Longsword; poleax
   - Location: The stream
   - Outcome: Both horses go down
5. **Thunder strikes Ser Lucas and the horses fall** — Wounded, Thunder rears and lashes out, one hoof catching Lucas in the face and another on the shoulder, then comes down atop the courser; both horses go down in a tangle.
   - Agent: Thunder
   - Patient: Ser Lucas, the chestnut courser
   - Location: The stream
   - Outcome: Both riders unhorsed into the water
6. **Dunk drowns and rises** — Dunk falls face-first, one foot caught in a stirrup, and the stream floods his helm; his leg nearly pulled from its socket by Thunder's struggles, he is briefly submerged, then gathers his legs and stands, having lost his sword.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Location: The stream
   - Outcome: Dunk survives but loses his sword and is injured
7. **Dunk kills Ser Lucas** — Lucas bursts up sword in hand and drives Dunk down, splitting his shield; Egg screams for Dunk to fight; Dunk tackles Lucas underwater, holds him down, draws his dagger, and drives it through the mail and leather beneath Lucas's arm, turning it until the strength leaves him.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Ser Lucas Inchfield
   - Instrument: Dagger
   - Location: The stream
   - Witness: Egg
   - Outcome: Ser Lucas's death
8. **Egg pulls Dunk from the stream and guards him** — Egg (himself drowned by the water's account, revived) pulls the drowned Dunk from the stream, helps remove his armor, rides with him in the wayn to Coldmoat, and sits by his side with the sword across his lap, making the maester taste Dunk's food.
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Location: The stream / Coldmoat
   - Outcome: Dunk brought to Coldmoat and guarded
9. **Maester Cerrick revives and treats Dunk** — The ironborn maester, drawing on Drowned God priests' methods, brings the drowned Dunk back and treats his broken ankle, sprained knee, broken collarbone, bruising, and facial gash.
   - Agent: Maester Cerrick
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Location: Coldmoat maester's tower
   - Outcome: Dunk revived and healing
10. **Ser Eustace and Lady Rohanne reconcile and wed** — After the battle, Rohanne asks leave to cross Eustace's land to visit Addam's grave, weeps before the blackberries, and Eustace comforts her; they talk all night of Addam and her father, and are wed that morning by Septon Sefton, making Eustace lord of Coldmoat.
    - Agent: Ser Eustace / Lady Rohanne
    - Location: Addam's grave / Coldmoat
    - Instigator: (marriage officiated by Septon Sefton)
    - Outcome: Marriage; Coldmoat and Standfast reconciled
11. **Rohanne offers Dunk a place and a horse; both refused** — In the stables Rohanne offers Dunk a captaincy of her guards, a place for Egg among her squires, and the blood-bay mare Flame/"Amends"; Dunk declines all, saying the mare is too fine for a bastard.
    - Agent: Lady Rohanne
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: Coldmoat stables
    - Outcome: Dunk refuses
12. **Dunk kisses Rohanne and cuts off her braid** — Dunk grabs Rohanne's braid, pulls her to him, kisses her, then draws his dagger and cuts off the braid as the keepsake he chooses instead of the horse.
    - Agent: Dunk
    - Patient: Lady Rohanne
    - Instrument: Dagger
    - Location: Coldmoat stables
    - Outcome: Dunk keeps a lock of her red hair
13. **Departure and the choice of road** — At the gatehouse Egg waits mounted on a new sorrel palfrey leading the mule; the two ride out across the drawbridge, pass the iron cage of corpses at the crossroads, and choose to turn north toward the Wall rather than south to Summerhall.
    - Agent: Dunk, Egg
    - Location: Coldmoat / the crossroads
    - Outcome: They ride north
